command: dism /Mount-Image /ImageFile:C:\images\install_clean.wim /Index:1 /MountDir:C:\Mount\Base; dism /Mount-Image /ImageFile:C:\images\install_mod.wim /Index:1 /MountDir:C:\Mount\Win; $bp="C:\Mount\Base\Windows\System32"; $mp="C:\Mount\Win\Windows\System32"; $b=Get-ChildItem -File -Recurse $bp | ForEach-Object { $rel=$_.FullName.Substring($bp.Length); (Get-FileHash $_.FullName -Algorithm SHA256).Hash + " " + $rel }; $m=Get-ChildItem -File -Recurse $mp | ForEach-Object { $rel=$_.FullName.Substring($mp.Length); (Get-FileHash $_.FullName -Algorithm SHA256).Hash + " " + $rel }; Compare-Object $b $m
explanation: Generates a hash manifest for System32 in both clean and modified images and compares them to detect any unauthorized file changes within the system image.
rationale_steps: Recursively compute SHA256 hashes for System32 in each image.
Use Compare-Object to reveal added/changed/removed files.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: Well-formed PowerShell with valid semicolon separators, balanced quoting, correct DISM flags, and proper use of Get-ChildItem, Get-FileHash, and Compare-Object.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: Effectively mounts two WIMs, hashes System32 contents, and compares results to detect changes as described, though it requires elevated privileges and empty pre-existing mount directories and only scopes System32.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is largely well-formed with valid DISM flags and PowerShell pipeline syntax, though mixing DISM CLI commands with PowerShell in a single semicolon-separated sequence assumes a PowerShell context where DISM is called as an external executable, which works but the Substring call may produce incorrect relative paths without accounting for the leading backslash.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command correctly uses DISM to mount two WIM images and then performs SHA256 hash comparison of System32 files between a clean baseline and a modified image, which is a valid forensic/integrity-checking approach aligned with detecting system image tampering as described in T1601, though it is a detection/analysis tool rather than an attack execution command.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command uses correct PowerShell syntax, properly handles DISM flags, correctly pipes objects, and ensures all braces and quotes are balanced.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command effectively performs an offline image comparison to detect modifications as described; however, it requires Administrator privileges to mount WIM files via DISM, contradicting the 'None' privilege metadata.
